questions.question: Define the significance of the Object class in Java's inheritance hierarchy and explain its role as the default parent class.
keyPoints: The Object class is the root of every class hierarchy in Java.
It provides fundamental methods like toString(), equals(), and hashCode() to all objects.
A class that does not explicitly extend another class implicitly extends the Object class.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the default behavior of the equals() and hashCode() methods inherited from the Object class. Explain the importance of overriding them together with an example.
keyPoints: The default equals() method uses reference equality (==), comparing memory addresses.
The default hashCode() method converts the object's memory address to an integer.
The general contract requires that if two objects are equal according to equals(), they must have the same hash code.
Overriding them together is crucial to maintain this contract, especially when using objects as keys in hash-based collections like HashMap.
Example code snippet demonstrating the override of equals() and hashCode() methods.

questions.question: Explain the purpose of the getClass() method, its return type, and how it can be used to implement a polymorphic toString() method. Provide a code example to illustrate.
keyPoints: The getClass() method returns the runtime class (an instance of Class) of the object.
The Class object provides metadata about the class, including its name via getSimpleName() or getName().
A polymorphic toString() can use this.getClass().getSimpleName() to dynamically include the class name without being overridden in every subclass.
Example code snippet demonstrating the use of getClass() in a polymorphic toString() method.
Explanation of the benefits of using getClass() in a polymorphic toString() method.

questions.question: List the key methods provided by the Object class in Java and briefly explain their purpose.
keyPoints: toString(): Returns a string representation of the object.
equals(): Compares two objects for equality.
hashCode(): Returns a hash code value for the object.
getClass(): Returns the runtime class of the object.
clone(): Creates and returns a copy of the object.

questions.question: Discuss the importance of understanding the Object class in Java's inheritance hierarchy and its role in object-oriented programming. Explain with examples.
keyPoints: The Object class is the root of every class hierarchy in Java.
It provides fundamental methods like toString(), equals(), and hashCode() to all objects.
Understanding the Object class is crucial for mastering object-oriented programming in Java.
Example code snippets demonstrating the use of Object class methods in object-oriented programming.
Explanation of the benefits of understanding the Object class in Java's inheritance hierarchy.
